body:
### Problem

Since #39625 has been merged, I think I'd document the intention and (tentative) roadmap for the refactoring of `matchparen.vim`/`matchit.vim`.

1. matchit implements extensions for `%` matching so that it is not limited to brackets, while matchparen implements the highlighting of brackets. This leads to:
   - The matching logic is being implemented redundantly in both places.
   - Matches extended by matchit are not highlighted by matchparen.
2. Maches is controlled by Vim regex and is based on text matching rather than language syntax; consequently, we must deal with a large number of edge cases.
3. Currently, they are written in VimScript, which makes it difficult for us to provide solutions to the aforementioned issues or implement further improvements within this repository.

### Expected behavior

One by one:

1. Let matchit and matchparen share part of the logic to reduce code while making `%` achieve "what you see is what you get."
2. Provide a matching method based on tree-sitter, which can reduce a lot of maintenance burden, while retaining support for legacy regex as much as possible.
3. Rewrite in Lua.

### Roadmap
- [x] Rewrite `matchparen.vim` in Lua. #39625
- [ ] Rewrite `matchit.vim` in Lua
- [ ] Refactor a common module to share matching logic between the two plugins
- [ ] Provide tree-sitter support

comment_data.body: See the previous discussion at https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/31563 (which already added rudimentary treesitter support)

The support it added is to avoid adding matches within certain tree-sitter nodes. What I'm envisioning here is that since we can define matchit via regex, why not also allow defining matchit using tree-sitter queries? I think providing equivalent tree-sitter support for places that currently use vim regex is a reasonable improvement.

However, given the current maintenance model, it's unlikely that these queries would be provided by core. And the feasibility of such a feature would depend on the difficulty of implementation and maintenance.

Yes, I know. The point of the reference is to repeat my strong recommendation to piggy-back on the highlight queries, not reinvent your own (for exactly that reason). This does not preclude adding _additional_ support for custom queries down the road, but the baseline should be `@delimiter` and `@keyword.conditional` as this gives a broad base.

> The point of the reference is to repeat my strong recommendation to piggy-back on the highlight queries, not reinvent your own (for exactly that reason).

This approach is attractive, and I will explore this direction later. I'm just not sure whether we can use the same capture groups for every language.

We can refer to the `textDocument/documentHighlight` in LSP, as it serves a similar purpose. Even though it usually highlights variable references in a document, most LSPs have implemented matching functionality. For example, when the cursor is on `function`, it highlights `end`; when the cursor is on `if ... then ... else ... end`, it highlights each node, while users can jump between these highlights via keymaps, very similar to how we want matchit and matchparen to achieve. I have a PR for this purpose. #30841

If we change the meaning of "matching" here to make it closer to the effect of document highlight, it might be feasible and more effective to fuse the highlight queries.

With matchparen and matchit getting rewritten in Lua, it might be worth seeing if these plugin' functionality could be upgraded from plugins into parts of Nvim core:
- For matchparen, it could be changed from a plugin to an option (e.g. `'matchparen'`), so `:DoMatchParen`/`:NoMatchParen` become `:set matchparen`/`:set nomatchparen`.
- Matchit has much more complexity to it, so I think it makes sense to keep it as a plugin. However, it's definitely still possible to make this native. It could be as simple as a `'matchit'` option to enable/disable it (and potentially a `'matchitoptions'` option for any needed configuration). The barrier here is language support, since we wouldn't want that to be hardcoded. If we only use treesitter and drop regex support, that's easy. Ideally if Nvim controlled ftplugins (which are sadly Vim's), they could even set an option like `'matchitregex'` per filetype, but alas, that doesn't work.

Yes, that's the long-term goal:
* extract base (shared, reusable) functionality into core
* as these are enabled by default in Nvim (as opposed to Vim?), we can "bake" them into the runtime more aggressively.

We still need a (per-buffer) optout, but this need not be a command (or an option; the variable approach works well enough from Lua as well -- personally, I'm hesitant to move more things into the option subsystem before the big overhaul that will allow for richer option types).

I don't think we want to drop regex support in general; long-term we want to be treesitter first, but that needs to be a sliding migration.

Luckily, the situation with ftplugins is not quite as dire as you think since Vimscript and Lua plugins can exist in parallel and are _both_ sourced in a defined order (with the latter clearly owned by Neovim). So we can absolutely layer our own runtime files on top of Vim's, as long as they don't conflict. We already do this for the filetypes we enable treesitter for by default.

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39683#issuecomment-4408438743

> We can refer to the `textDocument/documentHighlight` in LSP, as it serves a similar purpose. Even though it usually highlights variable references in a document, most LSPs have implemented matching functionality.

I've been experimenting with this exact idea recently.

Overloading `%` to jump to the next LSP highlight (I'm using the [`Snacks.words`](https://github.com/folke/snacks.nvim/blob/main/docs/words.md) plugin) if possible and falling back to `matchit`. The added benefit of `documentHighlight` is that it allows you to cycle through more than just the delimiter keywords like `do`/`end`. Including `break`/`continue` in loops, `return` statements in functions, and the final expression of a block in expression-oriented languages.

If both `matchit` Lua rewrite and `vim.lsp.document_highlight.jump()` from #30841 were to get implemented it might make sense to tightly integrate them together like this similar to how `v_an` and `v_in` support both TS and LSP.

(As the incremental selection feature demonstrates, treesitter and LSP support is not mutually exclusive but complementary -- albeit tricky to get right -- so it absolutely fits into our design goals to have regex, treesitter, _and_ LSP support for `matchfoo` as well.)

What is the point of this commit? Haven't found any explanation why this is required for a plugin that should only works in the current window/buffer.
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39625/changes/f0f6753f30905c221007579487059ad959f14a30

> What is the point of this commit? Haven't found any explanation why this is required for a plugin that should only works in the current window/buffer.

This is not true; you can see the vimscript version of matchparen:
https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/dfd6cd477fc3713b70c167c2a991ccc99af66d3f/runtime/plugin/matchparen.vim#L216-L229

`wincall`/`windo`/`doau CursorMoved` were used precisely to make it work in other windows, and for that purpose, it also used `noau`/`silent` to avoid side effects. The current version directly passes the window ID to `highlight_matching_pair`, which is much more intuitive.

Furthermore, this is part of making the code "more nvim-like," as most Neovim functions provide `win`/`buf` as arguments rather than implicitly relying on the concept of "current." This is essentially a feature: we can now support windows other than the current one, which will facilitate further refactoring in the future.

Do you understand what this functions do? They are called only once on enabling/disabling the plugin. And now your version is unnecessary complicated and my bet it's performance is even worse in some hot pathes like skip function invoking "api.nvim_win_call()".

You just translated old vim version burden into "new" lua implementation without thinking and added more questionable "improvements".
Not sure why it were merged.

Why do you need highlight matching pairs at current cursor position in "other" windows? Nice "feature".

> Do you understand what this functions do? They are called only once on enabling/disabling the plugin.

This is also not true; these two functions are used to provide the `:DoMatchParen` and `:NoMatchParen` commands, and they can be called, and they can be called any number of times.

Please stop adding off-topic comments here. Also, you lost the bet; see https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39681#issuecomment-4412026538 for performance, and see https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39636 for a feature request to reduce `win_call`. We did think things through when making changes and adding comments, unlike you.

Not sure who thought it was offtopic because the title is `tracking: migrate matchparen.vim/matchit.vim to Lua and improvements`. And my first question was about matchparen.vim improvement.

> This is also not true; these two functions are used to provide the `:DoMatchParen` and `:NoMatchParen` commands, and they can be called, and they can be called any number of times.

But they are not called on every cursor move.

> > Also, you lost the bet; see [#39681 (comment)](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39681#issuecomment-4412026538) for performance

Not sure if I do believe it, because bottlenecks are "synstack()" (for treesitter it would be get_captures()) and "searchpairpos()" functions, and you have not changed them. You have only benchmarked brackets which are close to each other like this `()`?
Benchmark this file https://gist.github.com/monkoose/24dbced1234092c7bf186fd2f6875d69

@monkoose Since this is a 'tracking' issue, I believe the scope of this particular thread is best restricted to general directions and roadmaps. Directly leaving a comment at https://github.com/neovim/neovim/commit/f0f6753f30905c221007579487059ad959f14a30 or https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39625 via code review would be more appropriate for questions about the concrete implementation and discussing the details.

I also believe it would be possible to convey the same questions using less heated language, which actually makes conversations more efficient in most cases. The questions themselves appear valid to me, but let's try to keep things civil.
@WieeRd Yes, sorry, my second reply is harsh. It is also partly because of the language barrier.

@monkoose If you found a performance regression, you should open a new issue with a reproduction. If you read the description and discussion of this issue, you would know that the changes we are going to make are much more extensive; porting to Lua is just a start, and many of the implementations here (including `win_call`) are not intended to be kept long-term, and once again, parameterizing `win` is because such a refactor will be useful in the future.

> And my first question was about matchparen.vim improvement.

No, your first question was a (rather rude -- I know there is a language barrier, so take this as constructive learning feedback) comment about a specific merged commit. That does not belong here.

I'm going to (temporarily) lock this thread to cool off.

labels.description: changes that are not features or bugfixes
labels.name: refactor

milestone.description: Low priority items. We plan to work on this but don't have a target date.
milestone.title: backlog

title: tracking: migrate `matchparen.vim`/`matchit.vim` to Lua and improvements
